questions.text: When a new potential client reaches out, what does your first meaningful response to them typically look like?

options.text: I ask questions about their goals, timeline, and budget to learn what I need — the fit becomes clear through the conversation.
options.label: A

options.text: I give them an overview of how I work and my general pricing range, then ask a few qualifying questions.
options.label: B

options.text: I send them through a defined intake process — a document or set of criteria that tells me quickly whether we're a match.
options.label: C

questions.text: When a prospect asks *"what do you actually charge for this kind of project?"* — where does your answer come from?

options.text: From the conversation itself. I listen to their scope, their context, and name a number that feels right for this engagement.
options.label: A

options.text: From my experience with similar projects — I have a sense of my market rate that I adapt based on the specifics.
options.label: B

options.text: From a pricing structure I've already built. The number exists before the conversation starts.
options.label: C

questions.text: The last time a prospect asked for your rates and process — how did you actually respond?

options.text: I walked them through it on the call — that conversation is where the offer gets defined for their specific situation.
options.label: A

options.text: I sent something I'd used before and adjusted it to fit what they were asking about.
options.label: B

options.text: I sent a document that already existed. It answered their questions without a follow-up being needed.
options.label: C

questions.text: If a referral partner asked *"why should I send someone your way instead of the other three people I know who do similar work?"* — where would your answer come from?

options.text: From what I know about my own work — I'd explain what makes my approach different based on what I've seen work for clients.
options.label: A

options.text: From something I've articulated before, though it varies depending on who's asking and what they need to hear.
options.label: B

options.text: From a written statement I've built specifically for this — specific, consistent, the same every time I use it.
options.label: C

questions.text: Think about the last project that created scope tension, revision friction, or a mismatch in what the client expected. When did you first sense the fit wasn't right?

options.text: Early on — there was usually a signal in the first conversation — but I made a call to move forward and managed it.
options.label: A

options.text: Once the project was underway and requests started stretching beyond what I'd planned for.
options.label: B

options.text: I almost never have this experience. The projects I take on are well-matched to what I do.
options.label: C

questions.text: When a task comes in from a client — a revision, a new deliverable, a *"quick thing"* — what happens to it?

options.text: It goes into my mental queue. I deal with it when I get to that client's work.
options.label: A

options.text: I note it somewhere — in the email thread, a sticky note, or a notes app — and try to action it before it falls through.
options.label: B

options.text: It goes into a central place immediately, connected to that client's project.
options.label: C

questions.text: If I asked you right now to list every open deliverable for your most active client — where would that answer come from?

options.text: My knowledge of the project. I'm close to this work and know where things stand.
options.label: A

options.text: A combination of checking recent emails and pulling together what I remember.
options.label: B

options.text: A single record I'd open. Everything is listed there with its current status.
options.label: C

questions.text: On a typical Monday morning — after a weekend where clients may have emailed, decisions may have been made, things may have moved — how long before you have an accurate, complete picture of where every active client stands?

options.text: That picture comes together gradually as I work through messages. I build it from what came in while I was away.
options.label: A

options.text: 15–20 minutes — once I've gone through email and messages I usually have what I need.
options.label: B

options.text: Almost immediately — I have something that shows me the current state before I open a single message.
options.label: C

questions.text: When you sit down to work on a typical morning — before you've acted on anything yet — how do you actually know what to work on first across all your active clients?

options.text: I work through what's in front of me — most urgent email, most recent message, whatever's been waiting longest.
options.label: A

options.text: I have a general sense of the week's priorities and I start from there, checking in on things as I go.
options.label: B

options.text: I look at something that shows me an organized view of all active clients and their most pressing items, then decide.
options.label: C

questions.text: When you added your last new client, what changed about how you worked day-to-day?

options.text: I had to figure out how to fit them in. It took some adjustment to accommodate a new set of deliverables alongside what already existed.
options.label: A

options.text: My workload increased and things got harder to track, but I managed.
options.label: B

options.text: The new client joined what was already there. The structure didn't need to change.
options.label: C

questions.text: Think about your last three weeks of content. What determined which weeks you published and which weeks you didn't?

options.text: Whether I had something worth sharing and the time to share it. Some weeks both aligned, some weeks neither did.
options.label: A

options.text: Mostly my client workload — light weeks I posted more, heavy weeks I didn't.
options.label: B

options.text: A schedule I follow regardless of what else is happening.
options.label: C

questions.text: In the last 30 days, what most determined how much content you actually published?

options.text: What inspired me or came up in my work that felt worth sharing.
options.label: A

options.text: How busy I was with client work — heavier client weeks usually meant content stopped, not just slowed.
options.label: B

options.text: A publishing plan I hold to regardless of what else is happening.
options.label: C

questions.text: A strong month of content. Then a strong month of new inquiries. Did you ever know whether the first caused the second?

options.text: I noticed both happened in the same period, but I couldn't tell whether the content drove the inquiries or whether it was something else.
options.label: A

options.text: I had a strong feeling the content helped — some people mentioned specific posts — but I couldn't measure how much.
options.label: B

options.text: Yes. I track both and the connection was visible in the data.
options.label: C

questions.text: The last time you published a piece of content that got strong engagement — did you publish more in that direction afterward?

options.text: Not specifically — I noticed the engagement but the next piece came from something different that came up.
options.label: A

options.text: I tried to — I made a note about it but didn't have a systematic way to follow through.
options.label: B

options.text: Yes. I have a way to capture what performed and feed it into what I create next.
options.label: C

questions.text: In your last slow month for new client inquiries, did you increase your content output — and if you did, did it make a measurable difference?

options.text: I posted more, and the month after did seem busier, but I couldn't tell if content caused it.
options.label: A

options.text: I meant to post more but the stress of a slow period actually made it harder to create.
options.label: B

options.text: I tracked it and could see the correlation between content activity and inquiry volume.
options.label: C

questions.text: Right now, if someone asked you how many potential clients you're actively in conversations with — how would you answer?

options.text: From memory — I'd give them a number and maybe check a couple of recent threads to make sure I'm not forgetting anyone.
options.label: A

options.text: I know roughly — the active ones are the people I'm currently emailing or talking to.
options.label: B

options.text: I'd open my pipeline and give them an accurate count with each person's current stage.
options.label: C

questions.text: Think about the last three people you reached out to as potential clients. What determined that you'd reach out to them specifically?

options.text: Something about them felt like a natural fit — their work, their profile, a mutual connection.
options.label: A

options.text: They came up in my network at a moment when I was actively thinking about finding new work.
options.label: B

options.text: They matched criteria I have for the kind of client I want to be working with.
options.label: C

questions.text: Your last closed client — if someone asked right now how they found you, what would you say?

options.text: What they told me in conversation — they mentioned it and I remember it.
options.label: A

options.text: My best guess — I'm fairly sure I know but I didn't write it down.
options.label: B

options.text: What's in my pipeline — I log where clients come from so I can verify it.
options.label: C

questions.text: When you think about which channels have consistently brought you clients — how well do you actually understand which ones are working?

options.text: I have a general sense — referrals, content, direct outreach — but the specifics across channels aren't something I've measured.
options.label: A

options.text: I've noticed that most clients seem to come through referrals, and maybe one other channel seems active — but I couldn't break it down further or verify it.
options.label: B

options.text: I track where clients come from, so I can tell you which channels are performing and which aren't.
options.label: C

questions.text: When you're in a quiet period for new client inquiries — how do you decide what to do about it?

options.text: I think about who I know who might need something and reach out to a few people, maybe post more content.
options.label: A

options.text: I reflect on what's worked before and try to do more of that, even if I'm going on memory.
options.label: B

options.text: I look at what the data shows has driven clients and work from that.
options.label: C

questions.text: For your most active client right now — if you needed to get completely current before a call in 30 minutes, where would you go first?

options.text: Their email thread. That's where the actual conversation, decisions, and file exchanges live.
options.label: A

options.text: A combination of places — email, a file I have somewhere, and memory to fill the gaps.
options.label: B

options.text: A single record I maintain for them. Everything is in there.
options.label: C

questions.text: How confident are you that you could give any active client a complete, accurate project status update within 10 minutes — without asking them to remind you of any details?

options.text: *(1 = Not at all confident —— 5 = Completely confident)*

questions.text: The last time a client asked for a project status update — what did that experience actually reveal about how your client records work?

options.text: That I know this project well. My awareness of where things stand was current enough to answer directly.
options.label: A

options.text: That I'm getting closer. I had most of it but needed to pull a few things together before replying.
options.label: B

options.text: That the system works. I opened one record and sent everything they needed in under 10 minutes.
options.label: C

questions.text: Think about the last time a client wanted to add something to a project mid-way through. How did it get handled?

options.text: We talked it through and got aligned on the call. I trusted the relationship to hold.
options.label: A

options.text: I noted it somewhere and adjusted my plan, even if the original brief didn't formally change.
options.label: B

options.text: I updated the scope document, confirmed it in writing, and adjusted the timeline.
options.label: C

questions.text: There are two ways a client operation can work. In one, **you are the connection between all the parts** — you carry information from email to tracker to invoice to memory. In the other, **the parts connect to each other**, and an update in one place flows through automatically. Which one describes your client operation right now?

options.text: The first one. I'm the link. When something changes, I carry it forward.
options.label: A

options.text: Somewhere between — I have some structure but I'm still the one maintaining most of the connections manually.
options.label: B

options.text: The second one. Updates flow. I don't maintain the connection — the record does.
options.label: C

questions.text: If your most active client emailed right now asking for a full rundown of where every deliverable stands and what they've paid so far — how long would it take you to send an accurate reply?

options.text: A few minutes. I know this project well and can write it from current knowledge.
options.label: A

options.text: 10–15 minutes. I'd want to verify a few things before sending to make sure it's complete.
options.label: B

options.text: Under 5 minutes. I open one record and have the complete picture.
options.label: C

questions.text: For your active clients right now — which of the following currently exist in one connected location? *(Select all that apply)*

options.text: Client contact information and communication history
Project brief and scope of work
Deliverable tracker with status per item
Invoice and payment status linked to the project
File and asset storage linked to the client record
Notes from calls, decisions, and approvals

questions.text:
Here is what Monday morning looks like for an operator with a connected client record.
A client emails at 9:03am asking for a complete update: where every deliverable stands, what's been approved, what's still outstanding, and what they owe.
The operator opens one page. The client's name is at the top. Directly below it: the scope they signed off on in week one. A tracker showing each deliverable — approved, in-progress, or outstanding. The invoice from three weeks ago, flagged because payment hasn't arrived yet. A note from last Thursday's call: *"Client asked for one more revision on the brand mark before final approval."*
The operator reads for 90 seconds. Sends a complete, accurate reply at 9:07am.
Now place your most active client there. Last Monday.
**If that same email had arrived — how would your next 30 minutes have looked?**

options.text: I would have opened their email thread, gone back through recent messages, checked my invoice tool, tried to remember the conversation from Thursday. I would have sent something in 20–30 minutes and still been slightly uncertain about one or two things.
options.label: A

options.text: I would have had most of it but needed to verify the invoice status or confirm one deliverable detail. Maybe 10–15 minutes, one check.
options.label: B

options.text: I have that record. I would have opened one page and sent a complete reply in under 10 minutes.
options.label: C

questions.text: Of the components you just identified as not currently in one connected location — have you had a version of this organized before, in any tool or template, even if you stopped using it?

options.text: Yes, multiple times. Something always makes it stop working after a few weeks.
options.label: A

options.text: I've started building something like this once or twice but never finished it.
options.label: B

options.text: No — this kind of connected record would be new territory for me.
options.label: C

questions.open_text_label: If you selected A or B — in a few words, what do you think made it stop working?

questions.text: When a new client engagement starts and the scope is being defined — where does your pricing come from?

options.text: From the conversation. I listen to the scope and the client and name a number that feels right for this engagement.
options.label: A

options.text: From my general sense of market rate, which I apply with some flexibility depending on the project.
options.label: B

options.text: From a defined structure I've built. The pricing exists before the conversation starts.
options.label: C

questions.text: Without opening your email or your bank right now — how accurately could you describe your outstanding invoices?

options.text: Very accurately. I keep track of who owes me because it matters and I pay close attention.
options.label: A

options.text: Roughly. I know the significant ones but I'd want to verify the smaller or older ones.
options.label: B

options.text: Exactly. I have a tracker that shows me the status of every invoice at any time.
options.label: C

questions.text: A project ran longer than you planned due to revisions. Once it was finished — did you actually know whether it was profitable?

options.text: I had a clear enough sense — I know roughly what it cost me in time versus what it paid.
options.label: A

options.text: I could piece it together if I needed to, but I didn't calculate it formally.
options.label: B

options.text: Yes. I track enough to calculate per-project profitability after every engagement.
options.label: C

questions.text: On the first of every month — what does your financial picture actually look like?

options.text: I check my bank and know where I stand for the month.
options.label: A

options.text: I look at what's outstanding and try to get a rough sense of what's coming in over the next few weeks.
options.label: B

options.text: I have a current view — revenue, outstanding invoices, projected income — maintained and ready to review.
options.label: C

questions.text:
Right now — which of the following exist in your financial system? *(Select all that apply)*
## Deployment Notes
<aside>
⚙️
**Q6.5 open text** must be captured before the results page renders. The answer personalizes which Pattern Paragraph version fires.
**Pattern Paragraph versions (results page):**
- Version A fires if Q6.5 = A (multiple failed attempts)
- Version B fires if Q6.5 = B (started, never finished)
- Version C fires if Q6.5 = C (first attempt)
**OCI totals** (Section 5 + Section 6 checklists) feed a component gap count on the results page: *"You have X of 12 operational components in one connected location."*
**CRGM score** (Q2a) surfaces on results page alongside section scores — not folded into Section 5 total.
**Zeigarnik gate:** Show section scores and total score only. Do not show tier label, interpretation, or recommendation before email capture. The score number creates the pull — the meaning requires the email.
**Section 3 drop-off risk:** Consider a micro-progress indicator between Section 2 and Section 3: *"You're 35% through. Section 3 takes about 90 seconds."*
</aside>
## Score Tier Reference *(Backend — Not Shown to User Before Email)*
| Score | Tier | Pattern Paragraph Theme |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 0–15 | Reactive Operation | Everything runs through you personally. No architecture exists yet. |
| 16–30 | Partial Infrastructure | Components exist but aren't connected. Manual maintenance everywhere. |
| 31–45 | Emerging Architecture | Structure is forming. Key connections still missing or inconsistent. |
| 46–55 | Near-Operational | Most infrastructure in place. Gap is integration and reliability. |
| 56–60 | Connected Operation | Fully operational. Gap is optimization and scale. |
[KineticOS Interactive Diagnostic Report Engine — System Architecture & Thinking Framework](https://app.notion.com/p/KineticOS-Interactive-Diagnostic-Report-Engine-System-Architecture-Thinking-Framework-6180fe8d0c5e83ff9b26811801af147a?pvs=21)

options.text: Documented pricing per service
Invoice tracker (sent / paid / overdue)
Monthly revenue history for the past 6 months
Expense tracking by project or month
Per-project profitability data
30-day projected revenue from current pipeline
